steps.text: Peer review the radiochemical method of analysis.
Pursue formal validation phases.
Ensure proper sample preservation and storage stability studies were performed during method development.
Investigate the stability of the analyte(s) from the time of sampling through the time of analysis.
Investigate the extract stability.
Calculate a combined standard uncertainty by propagating the standard uncertainties of the measured values used to calculate the result.
Multiply the combined standard uncertainty by a coverage factor to obtain an expanded uncertainty.
Include rigorous evaluations of trueness in EPA method validation studies.
Evaluate trueness minimally at the extremes of the quantitation range stated in the scope of a method, and at regulatory levels.
Test appropriate matrix blanks and fortified (spiked) matrix blanks to evaluate method specificity.
Test a worst-case sample matrix, as defined in the scope of a method, during both intra- and interlaboratory validation studies.
Study actual samples of material containing an undetermined amount of the inherent radionuclide to evaluate method selectivity.

Use multiple analytical procedures (e.g., sample preparation, wet-chemistry, and/or instrumental analysis procedures) during an intralaboratory validation study to substantiate the specificity of the method being evaluated.
Conduct ruggedness testing.
Identify critical control points in the documented method.
Initiate an interlaboratory method validation study.
Publish the EPA method for general use.
Verify MDC estimates by analyzing samples containing radionuclide at the claimed detection level.
Characterize analytical measurement uncertainty over an analyte concentration range.
Evaluate method ruggedness during method validation.
Include the design and results of the interlaboratory study in the method validation summary report.
Report data from the interlaboratory study in tabular form.
Include a discussion describing the details of, and rationale for, any changes made to the method resulting from the interlaboratory study.
Peer review EPA methods of analysis in accordance with the most current version of the EPA Science Policy Council’s Peer Review Handbook.

preconditions.text: Prior to publication of the radiochemical method of analysis.
Ensure substantial evidence exists that a method is a good candidate for publication as an EPA method.
If adequate representations of matrix blanks cannot be obtained or prepared
Ruggedness testing must be completed before initiating an interlaboratory method validation study.
Critical control points must be identified in the documented method before initiating an interlaboratory method validation study.
An interlaboratory method validation study must be completed prior to publishing an EPA method for general use.

constraints.text: If the analysis is based on the molecular form that the radionuclide is in, special precautions must address the chemical stability of that molecule and not necessarily the oxidation state of the radionuclide.
Microorganisms have the potential to oxidize or reduce certain radionuclides, and can introduce by-products that will induce chemical changes.

The reference materials approach introduces the important uncertainty of matrix matching, and the reliability of a bias estimate depends upon the relationship between the composition of the reference material and the samples.
When using the alternate method approach, the reliability of a bias estimate is dependent upon how much is known about the performance characteristics of the alternate method.
The matrix spiking approach introduces uncertainty regarding the behavior of spiked materials, compared to materials containing native analyte, particularly for solid materials.

Matrix blanks should contain the chemical species and inherent radionuclides (e.g., naturally occurring radionuclides), other than the radionuclide(s) of interest, which are reasonably expected to be present in an actual sample.
A combination of procedures or techniques that are based on different measurement principles should be used.
The fractional factorial experimental design referenced herein is not intended for method optimization purposes.
Changes in operating or environmental conditions during ruggedness testing should reflect expected, reasonable variations likely to be encountered in different laboratories.
